items.html:
The law behind your records

Almost every record you keep contains personal data — information that identifies a living person. In the UK this information is protected by the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018. These laws do not exist to make your job harder; they set out how personal data must be handled so the people you support keep control of information about their own lives.

The regulator for data protection is the Information Commissioner’s Office, known as the ICO. Your employer is responsible for following the law, but every entry you make is part of how your organisation meets it.

The data protection principles

The UK GDPR sets out core principles for handling personal data. In your everyday recording they mean you should:

  • Lawfulness, fairness and transparency: have a proper reason to record, and be open about it.
  • Purpose limitation: use information only for the care purpose it was gathered for.
  • Data minimisation: record what is relevant and necessary, not everything you know.
  • Accuracy: keep records correct and up to date.
  • Storage limitation: keep records only as long as they are genuinely needed.
  • Integrity and confidentiality: keep the information secure at all times.

Health and care records also count as special category data, which needs extra protection because it is so sensitive.

items.question: Under the data protection principle of data minimisation, what should you record in a person’s care notes?

answers.text: Only the information that is relevant and necessary for their care.
answers.feedback: Correct. Data minimisation means recording what is needed for the care purpose, not everything you happen to know.

answers.text: Everything you have learned about the person, in case it proves useful one day.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Recording more than is needed goes against data minimisation and can expose private details unnecessarily.

answers.text: As little as possible, even if some relevant care details are left out.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Records must still be complete enough for safe care; minimisation is about relevance, not leaving gaps.

answers.text: Whatever the person’s family would like to see included.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. What you record is guided by the care purpose and the law, not by what others would prefer to add.

items.html:
Consent and being open

People have a right to know what information is held about them and, in most cases, to have a say in how it is used. Being transparent is both part of the law and part of treating someone with respect. The people you support should never feel that records are being kept about them in secret.

When you gather information, explain simply why you need it and what will happen to it. A person who understands and agrees is far more likely to share the details that help you support them well.

Seeing their own records

Under data protection law, a person has the right to ask for a copy of the personal information held about them. This is called a subject access request, and it can be made either verbally or in writing. It is one of the strongest rights the law gives people.

  • Anyone can ask: a person does not have to give a reason for the request.
  • Usually free: a copy is normally provided without any charge.
  • There is a time limit: your organisation must respond within the period set out in law.
  • Pass it on: if someone asks to see their records, report the request to your manager straight away.

Writing clear, factual and respectful records matters all the more when the person themselves may one day read them.

items.question: If a person you support asks to see the information held about them, they must give a reason and put the request in writing before it can be considered.
items.correctMsg: Correct. A subject access request can be made verbally or in writing, and the person does not have to give any reason.
items.wrongMsg: Not quite. A subject access request needs no reason and can be made verbally as well as in writing, so it should always be taken seriously.

items.html:
Two ways of keeping records

Care records may be kept on paper, in an electronic care planning system, or a mix of both. Whichever your workplace uses, the same duty applies: information must be accurate, up to date and kept secure. The format changes the practical risks, not the underlying principle.

Paper records can be lost, left open on a side, or read by the wrong person. Electronic records can be seen by anyone who gets into an unlocked account. Good everyday habits are what protect both.

Keeping devices and passwords safe

Many care workers now record on a phone, tablet or laptop, sometimes in a person’s own home. Handled carelessly, a small device can expose a great deal of personal data.

  • Keep passwords private: never share your login or write it on a sticky note.
  • Log out or lock: lock the screen whenever you step away from a device.
  • One person at a time: do not let colleagues use your account, because entries are linked to you.
  • Store paper safely: keep paper notes out of sight and return them to secure storage.
  • Report loss quickly: tell your manager at once if a device or file goes missing.

Your login is like your signature: whatever is recorded under it is treated as having been done by you.

items.question: You are recording on a shared work tablet and are called away to help another person. What is the safest thing to do with the tablet?

answers.text: Lock or log out of the tablet before you step away, then log back in when you return.
answers.feedback: Correct. Locking or logging out stops anyone else seeing personal data under your account while you are away.

answers.text: Leave it open on the care system so you can carry on quickly afterwards.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. An unlocked device leaves personal data on show to whoever passes by.

answers.text: Ask a colleague to finish your entry under your login while you are gone.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Entries are linked to your login, so nobody else should record under it.

answers.text: Hand the unlocked tablet to the person to hold for you.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. This exposes other people’s records and is not a safe way to secure the device.

items.html:
Confidentiality is not the same as secrecy

Confidentiality means personal information is shared only with those who have a genuine need to know it for the person’s care. It does not mean information is never shared at all. Sometimes sharing is exactly the right thing to do — for example, passing a change in someone’s condition to a nurse or GP.

The skill is knowing what to share, with whom, and why. Share too little and a person may not get safe care; share too widely and you break both their trust and the law.

The Caldicott principles

The Caldicott principles are a well-known guide for handling confidential health and care information. They help you judge when sharing is appropriate. In short, they remind you to:

  • Justify the purpose: be clear about why information is being shared.
  • Use only what is needed: share the minimum amount necessary.
  • Share on a need-to-know basis: only with those involved in the person’s care.
  • Respect duties both ways: the duty to share can matter as much as the duty to protect.
  • Follow the law: everyone handling information should understand their responsibilities.

A further principle reminds services to be open with people about how their information is used.

items.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these sentences about sharing information.
items.textField: *Confidentiality* means sharing personal information only with those who genuinely need it. Sharing on a *need-to-know* basis limits who can see a person’s details. The Caldicott principles say you should share the *minimum* amount of information necessary. Sometimes the duty to *share* is as important as the duty to protect. Being *open* with people about how their information is used helps build trust.

items.html:
What a data breach looks like

A personal data breach is any event where personal information is lost, stolen, seen by the wrong person, or destroyed by accident. It is not always dramatic. A care record emailed to the wrong address, a paper file left on a bus, or a private conversation overheard in a corridor can all be breaches.

Serious breaches must be reported to the ICO, sometimes within a tight time limit, so acting quickly really matters. You are not expected to make that legal judgement on your own — but you are expected to raise it without delay.

Putting it together

You finish a visit to Mrs Okafor and realise the daily notes you printed for handover are missing from your bag. You think you may have left them at the previous person’s home. What should you do?

  • Act quickly: tell your manager straight away rather than waiting to see if the notes turn up.
  • Be honest: explain exactly what happened, without playing it down.
  • Record the facts: note what was lost, when, and what you have done since.
  • Learn from it: agree how to carry records more safely in future.

Reporting your own mistake is not a failure — it is what protects Mrs Okafor and helps your organisation put things right.

items.question: You realise you may have left a person’s printed care notes at another address. What should you do first?

answers.text: Report it to your manager straight away and be honest about what happened.
answers.feedback: Correct. Reporting promptly and honestly lets your organisation act within any legal time limits and reduce the harm.

answers.text: Wait a day or two to see whether the notes are handed back before saying anything.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Waiting risks missing a reporting deadline and leaves the person’s data exposed for longer.

answers.text: Say nothing, as admitting the mistake could get you into trouble.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. Hiding a breach is far more serious than the loss itself and breaks your duty to be open.

answers.text: Deal with it yourself by retracing your route, without telling anyone.
answers.feedback: Incorrect. You may retrace your steps, but the breach must still be reported so it can be handled properly.
